owasp_top_10.title: OWASP Top 10 Web Application Security Risks
owasp_top_10.description: The OWASP Top 10 is a standard awareness document for web application security, representing the most critical security risks.

items.A01 Broken Access Control: Access control enforces policies so users cannot act outside their intended permissions. Failures lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of data. Common issues: IDOR, privilege escalation, CORS misconfig, forced browsing. Mitigation: deny by default, implement access control once and reuse, enforce record ownership, disable directory listing, log access control failures.
items.A02 Cryptographic Failures: Formerly 'Sensitive Data Exposure'. Failures related to cryptography that lead to exposure of sensitive data. Common issues: transmitting data in clear text, using weak/old crypto algorithms, not enforcing encryption, improper key management. Mitigation: classify data, don't store sensitive data unnecessarily, encrypt at rest & transit, use strong algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+), proper key management.
items.A03 Injection: User-supplied data is not validated, filtered, or sanitised by the application. Types: SQL injection, NoSQL, OS command, LDAP, XPath, template injection. Mitigation: use parameterised queries / prepared statements, use positive server-side input validation, escape special characters, use LIMIT in SQL to prevent mass disclosure.
items.A04 Insecure Design: Risks related to design and architectural flaws. Focus on threat modelling, secure design patterns, and reference architectures. Mitigation: establish secure development lifecycle, use threat modelling, integrate security language in user stories, integrate plausibility checks at each tier.
items.A05 Security Misconfiguration: Improperly configured permissions, unnecessary features enabled, default accounts/passwords, overly verbose error messages, missing security hardening. Mitigation: repeatable hardening process, minimal platform, review configurations, segmented architecture, send security directives to clients, automated verification.
items.A06 Vulnerable and Outdated Components: Using components with known vulnerabilities including libraries, frameworks, and software modules with known flaws. Mitigation: continuous inventory, monitor CVEs, obtain components from official sources, prefer signed packages, monitor unmaintained libraries.
items.A07 Identification and Authentication Fa: Weak authentication mechanisms, credential stuffing, brute force, weak passwords, improper session management. Mitigation: implement MFA, don't ship with default credentials, check weak passwords against top-10000 lists, harden against credential stuffing, limit failed login attempts.
items.A08 Software and Data Integrity Failures: Code and infrastructure that doesn't protect against integrity violations: using plugins/libraries from untrusted sources, insecure CI/CD pipelines, auto-update without integrity verification. Mitigation: use digital signatures, verify npm/Maven integrity, use tools like OWASP Dependency-Check, review code & config changes, ensure CI/CD has proper access control.
items.A09 Security Logging and Monitoring Fail: Without logging and monitoring, breaches cannot be detected. Insufficient logging, unclear log messages, logs only stored locally, no alerting. Mitigation: log all login, access control, and server-side input validation failures; use a format consumable by log management solutions; establish monitoring and alerting for suspicious activities.
items.A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF): SSRF flaws occur when a web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. Allows an attacker to coerce the app to send requests to unexpected destinations. Mitigation: sanitise and validate all URL input, enforce URL schema / port / destination with a positive allowlist, don't send raw responses to clients, disable HTTP redirections.
